title: AY19-20 Library Comprehensive Program Review-Dec2019 (Part 2)
text:
-- 1 of 24 --

2
Library staff reports to the Dean of Liberal Arts & Sciences in the Office of Instruction.
As our mission statement mentions, the Library primarily serves College of the Siskiyous students,
faculty, and employees. We secondarily serve our community members who are not currently enrolled
in classes.
The Library serves our campus by providing collections and resources relevant to campus needs.
Collection materials can be physical or electronic. Physical collections include books, reserve textbooks,
audio recordings in a variety of formats, DVDs, maps, periodicals, and ephemera. Electronic collections
include online articles, ebooks, and streaming videos; these are usually packaged together and accessed
as a database.
Resources are all the non-collection ways that the Library supports our campus and include library
employees and the variety of assistance we provide, online information, facilities, and equipment.
Library employees provide support of all kinds including reference/research assistance, computer tech
support, staff-mediated services such as interlibrary loan (ILL), and classroom instruction. The Library
manages the content on a portion of the COS web site where we provide general FAQs, self-serve how-
to information regarding research in the form of handouts and videos, and information to support
faculty.
As a facility, the Library offers quiet study space, a group study room, and a casual “lounge” area that
allows food and serves as an event space. The Library space also provides a home for our physical
collections as well as staff areas and storage. Our space contains necessary equipment such as
computers, printers (black & white and color), copier, scanner, microfilm reader, and media players.
The Library collaborates with classes across the curriculum to support student research needs. In the last
four years the Faculty Librarian has conducted 148 class visits in disciplines ranging from English to
Biology. Class visit content varies based on the needs of the research assignment. The Library reaches
an average of 1,240 students each academic year through these classes as well as tours, workshops, and
other activities. Library tours conducted as either part of a class or as a SOAR or other campus tour
totaled 66. The Library offered workshops through the Student Success Seminar series in the ASC for
several years until they were suspended. We reached additional students through 81 “other”
instructional activities such as offering general orientations for classes and the Lodges; being an
interactive class scavenger hunt stop; and the faculty librarian serving as in-class resource during hands-
on class research time.
AY15-16 AY16-17 AY17-18 AY18-19 totals
Class visits 40 35 25 48 148
Library tours 20 2 25 19 66
Workshops 8 0 0 0 8
Other 12 48 3 18 81
total 80 85 53 85 303
student participants reached 1,312 1,318 919 1,412 4,961

-- 2 of 24 --

title: AY19-20 Library Comprehensive Program Review-Dec2019 (Part 3)
text:
3
The Library looks for opportunities to build partnerships both on- and off-campus that will benefit both
the College and our community. Some recent examples include:
• Partnership with the COS Writers Club to host events: Writers Club Open Mic Night and Poetry
Night events regularly draw up to 50 attendees and is a great way to draw new people into the
Library. It gives our students a creative outlet for expression and helps them to build public
speaking skills and the courage to share their work and ideas. We hope that it also generates
excitement about literature, poetry, and reading! These public events support the College vision
for “cultural and community enrichment” and the Library’s mission to “serve the cultural needs
of our community.”
• Partnership with the Music Department: The Library has partnered with the Music program to
organize and re-house their instrumental sheet music. This will make the sheet music a more
usable collection and the Music program will not spend funds unnecessarily on sheet music they
already own.
• Partnership with the Siskiyou County Library: In order to leverage our very different collections
and limited budget, the Library now partners with the county library system, allowing books to
flow more easily between the two entities. Students can have public library materials sent to the
COS Library for pickup and public library patrons can have COS materials sent to their local
branch for pickup. COS students can also apply for a free public library card and have instant
access to all of the county library system’s online collections. The Library has also been a partner
for Siskiyou Reads, a non-profit organization affiliated with the public library system that
encourages all county residents to read and discuss a book in common.
• Participation in the statewide migration to new library software: In consultation with groups
around campus, the COS Library agreed to join 109 other California community college libraries
in migrating to a new library services platform (LSP). The chosen LSP is the same system used by
all CSU libraries, as well as SOU, OIT, Oregon State University libraries, and some libraries in the
University of California system. While each library will have local customizations, the system
works the same at all libraries, easing the transition for transferring students. It will also save
the College money for many years.1
The Library provides these resources and services to all students and employees year round with a small
budget that is only 1.1% of the College’s expenditures. Other than staff, our collections (primarily in
Capital Outlay), which include databases and the software required to manage them, are our largest
expenditures.
1 It is estimated that Library LSP costs will be approximately cut in half in FY20-21 and FY21-22, saving the college
about $13,000 annually. The LSP contract does not offer any firm costs after that date but costs should continue to
be much lower than if we had stayed with our current software system.

-- 3 of 24 --

2. External Factors with Significant Impact
The Library budget has been relatively flat for the last four years and we have only needed to make
minor cuts to cover our rising operating costs. We have been fortunate enough to receive some funding
from Instructional Equipment (AY17-18 for a new microfilm reader; AY19-20 for some new furniture),
Perkins (Spring 2018 to update our agriculture collection), and Foundation Mini-Grants (AY15-16:
marketing banner; AY17-18: portable display stands; AY18-19: sound system for Lounge events).
The Library has continued to lose personnel during the last four years. In November 2016, Nancy
Shepard, Assistant Dean of Learning Resources retired. As a dean overseeing several campus areas,
Nancy was necessarily less involved in day-to-day management than her predecessor, retired Library
Director Dennis Freeman, but her degree in Library Science and her office location in the Library has
meant that her presence is missed. Operating with just three staff members can be limiting; in
particular, with our one faculty librarian managing day-to-day operations since Nancy’s retirement,
instructional support can sometimes be limited. Projects these limitations have impacted include:
• Expansion of self-serve instructional web site content
• Expansion of content for and at the request of faculty
• Expansion of student employee training program in Canvas (possibly expanded to a “customer
service” badge students can put on their resumé)
• Creation of a credit-bearing research skills and/or information literacy course(s). These skills not
only help our students succeed as students but will help them meet employer expectations for
new employees.2
• Creation of cross-references in our collection to increase use of materials
• A greater physical presence on the Yreka Campus
The Library’s main rival in the realm of research is the Internet and the inaccurate (overly positive) self-
assessment many students have regarding their research skills. Both employer expectations and the
2 Head, Alison. Learning Curve: How College Graduates Solve Information Problems Once They Join the Workplace.
Project Information Literacy, 2012. https://www.projectinfolit.org/

-- 4 of 24 --

title: AY19-20 Library Comprehensive Program Review-Dec2019 (Part 5)
text:
5
need for library research skills for classroom success makes direct instructional opportunities and other
instructional outreach and support especially important.
3. Outcomes Assessment
During this time period the Library had three Student Learning Outcomes (SLOs). Below is each SLO
along with its assessment plan and assessment results.
SLO 1: Students who receive formal research instruction provided by the COS Library will
be able to effectively locate needed information using a variety of library resources.
Assessment: Various methods such as post-session evaluations, post-class surveys, and in-class
worksheets and activities; question on the Annual Student Survey
Time Frame for Assessment: Throughout the semester
How will we know this SLO has been met?: This SLO will have been successfully achieved when the most
recent Institutional Set Standards as agreed on by the Academic Senate are met as measured by:
• students answering post-session surveys “strongly agree” or “agree” to survey questions tied to
this SLO, or
• students completing worksheets are rated “✓+” or “✓”, or
• student grades on worksheets or assignments
The most recent Institutional Set Standards as agreed on by the Academic Senate:
• Transfer courses (such as ENGL 1001): ≥67%
• Credit courses (such as ENGL 0900): ≥67%
• Basic Skills courses (such as ENGL 0850): ≥45%
• Vocational Courses: ≥70%
Assessment Results:
Since AY15-16, the Library has collected assessments from 34 classes, primarily ENGL 0900 and ENGL
1001. During this time period the Library conducted evaluations after 25 class visits. Students
consistently report that the Library visits helped them to feel more confident in all of these areas,
meeting the institutional set standards. Post-class visit evaluations typically include questions similar to
the following, asking students to rate their confidence in their ability to find the research materials they
need for their assignments:
• The Library visits helped me to feel confident that I can get to the Library web site and
databases page
• The Library visits helped me to feel confident finding articles in EBSCO3
• The Library visits helped me to feel confident choosing keywords for a topic
• The Library visits helped me understand how search techniques such as using ““, *, and OR can
improve search results
3 EBSCO is the vendor who provides most of our article databases.

-- 5 of 24 --

title: AY19-20 Library Comprehensive Program Review-Dec2019 (Part 6)
text:
6
Students also actively demonstrated that they understood the concepts presented in class by
successfully completing worksheets in twelve classes.
The Library also teaches research skills to students one-on-one in the Library and through a variety of
educational materials on our Research Help page. This instruction is more difficult to assess. The Library
attempts to collect feedback through a question on our Annual Student Survey which is collected in the
Library, Student Center, and Lodges on the Weed Campus, the Main Office and/or ASC on the Yreka
Campus, and online (using a Survey Monkey link posted on our web site and shared with online
instructors). Results from this question show that the Library is meeting this SLO since 82-89% of
students feel that the Library helps them to develop their research skills.

SLO 2: Students who participate in formal research instruction will be able to evaluate for
credibility information provided by the library, Internet resources, and other information
sources.
Assessment/Time Frame for Assessment/How will we know this SLO has been met?: The Library does not
often have the chance to support this SLO in formal instruction sessions. It will be assessed
appropriately if the opportunity arises using the methods and standards outlined in SLO #1.
Assessment Results: Not assessed.
SLO 3: Students who use the COS Library and utilize its resources, services, and formal
instruction will appreciate the value of libraries in supporting academic and/or career
success and in supporting life-long learning.
Assessment: Question included on the library’s Annual Student Survey
Time Frame for Assessment: Around National Library Week in April (voluntary survey participation in the
Library, at other locations on the Weed and Yreka campuses, and online)
How will we know this SLO has been met?: This SLO will have been successfully achieved when 75% or
more of students surveyed “strongly agree” or “agree” to the Library Student Survey question about
appreciating the value of libraries.
Assessment Results: All survey results in the last six years show that we are easily and consistently
meeting this SLO: 88% - 100% of students “strongly agree” or “agree.”

Four-year assessment plan
The Library has had these same three SLOs for a very long time and we would like to reevaluate and
possibly revise or change them. There has been some discussion about whether the Library should have
student learning outcomes (SLOs) or program learning outcomes (PLOs) and the current Student
Learning Outcome Manual is in flux. We look forward to continued outcomes discussions. In the
meantime, we will continue to use our existing SLOs and will follow a similar assessment plan.
1. Students who receive formal research instruction provided by the COS Library will be able to
effectively locate needed information using a variety of library resources.
• Assessment: Various methods such as post-session evaluations, post-class surveys, and
in-class worksheets and activities; Annual Student Survey question
• Time Frame for Assessment: Throughout the semester
• How will we know this SLO has been met?: This SLO will have been successfully achieved
when the most recent Institutional Set Standards as agreed on by the Academic Senate
are met as measured by:
 students answering post-session surveys “strongly agree” or “agree” to survey
questions tied to this SLO, or
 students completing worksheets are rated “✓+” or “✓”, or
 student grades on worksheets or assignments
 ≥75% of Annual Student Survey respondents “strongly agree” or “agree”
The most recent Institutional Set Standards as agreed on by the Academic Senate:
 Transfer courses (such as ENGL 1001): ≥67%
 Credit courses (such as ENGL 0900): ≥67%
 Basic Skills courses (such as ENGL 0850): ≥45%
 Vocational Courses: ≥70%
2. Students who participate in formal research instruction will be able to evaluate for credibility
information provided by the library, Internet resources, and other information sources.
• Assessment/Time Frame for Assessment/How will we know this SLO has been met?: The
Library does not often have the chance to support this SLO in formal instruction
sessions. It will be assessed appropriately if the opportunity arises using the methods
and standards outlined in SLO #1.

8

appreciate the value of libraries in supporting academic and/or career success and in
supporting life-long learning.
• Assessment: Question included on the library’s annual student satisfaction survey
• Time Frame for Assessment: During National Library Week in April (voluntary
participation in the Library, at other locations on the Weed and Yreka campuses, and

• How will we know this SLO has been met?: This SLO will have been successfully achieved
when 75% or more of students surveyed “strongly agree” or “agree” to the Library
Student Survey question about appreciating the value of libraries.
4. Institutional Program Effectiveness Indicators
Not applicable or covered in other sections (especially 3 and 5).
5. Library Quantitative and Qualitative Data
The Library collects a wide variety of statistics. The following include some of the data that we have
found meaningful when looking at three broad areas of Library operations: our collections, research
services, and our physical space.
Use of the Library’s Collections
The Library tracks circulation statistics, which are the number of item checkouts each year. In general,
academic libraries are seeing overall circulation go down4 and COS is no exception. The trend is not as
grim as it first appears, however, since FTES have been declining during the same period: the number of
books circulations may have gone down but so has the number of students available to check them out.
Total circulation per credit FTES, including reserve textbooks, has actually remained fairly steady.5

title: AY19-20 Library Comprehensive Program Review-Dec2019 (Part 9)
text:
9
We think that recent reserve textbook use is decreasing in part because of the prevalence of book
voucher programs on campus through Siskiyou Promise and categorical programs such as EOPS. In
response to feedback from faculty and students, the Library worked for several years on our Program
Review goal of having a reserve textbook collection in Yreka. We tried to find categorical funds to pay
for these extra books but were unsuccessful. The Library now maintains a small collection of reserve
textbooks on the Yreka Campus from the Library’s budget. This collection has not been very successful,
however: since the collection was first made available in Fall 2018, only one book has ever been checked
out. The Library has done sporadic marketing of this collection but these dismal numbers indicate that
we need to find a better way to reach Yreka students or discontinue funding this collection.
The Library has done a better job at marketing our new books. In January 2018 the Library moved the
main service desk in order to free up a prime location to showcase new books and other displays.
Thanks to a Foundation Mini-Grant the Library was able to purchase new book display shelves to assist
in promoting items in this area. A simplistic look at circulation statistics show that 31 percent of new
books published between 2016-2019 (190 out of 616 titles) have circulated. By comparison, 13 percent
of our entire collection6 circulated during the same time period (3,701 circulations of 29,390 items).
These calculations are overly simplistic since some titles circulate more than once, some new books are
“checked out” for interlibrary loans, some exceptions are made and non-circulating titles are checked
out, etc. While it is a very rough comparison, it does seem to indicate that print is not dead at the COS
Library and that it is worthwhile to continue to buy and market new titles.
As use of physical books declines, the use of online resources has grown. Our largest online collections
(not including article databases) are the ebook collections Ebook Central (49,288 titles), EBSCO eBook
Collection (21,336 titles), and Credo Reference (917 reference book titles) and the streaming video
collection Films On Demand (36,138 film titles). Films On Demand (FOD) saw a significant jump in use
during the time period when COS switch from Etudes to Canvas for our online courses. The Library

In January 2018, the Library started using the same login system for off-campus access to our online
collections that the college uses for mySiskiyous and Canvas. This appears to have simplified off-campus
access for students since we no longer receive a steady stream of inquiries regarding specific difficulties
or the process in general. While this does not appear to have an impact on ebooks use, it may be the
explanation for the general spike in use of article databases between AY16-17 and AY17-18. The use
spike of Academic Search Complete is the most prominent, with a 28% increase in use between AY16-17
and AY17-18 and another 22% increase between AY17-18 and AY18-19.

title: AY19-20 Library Comprehensive Program Review-Dec2019 (Part 11)
text:
11
Agreeing or Agreeing. The Library currently spends about half of its budget (excluding personnel costs)
on materials for our collections. About 75 percent of collection costs are for online subscriptions and 25
percent for physical materials (25-30% of which is spent on reserve textbooks).8 Online collections are
cost effective and are readily available to all students 24/7, regardless of location. Physical titles are
carefully selected to target student needs and current interests, help fill gaps in our ebook collections (it
often takes 1-2 years for new titles to be included in our ebook collections), and fulfill student requests
for specific titles. This balance appears to be effective and helps us to meet Accreditation Standard IIB1.
2014 2015 2016 2017 2018 2019
Library collections are
sufficient in quantity,
currency, depth, and
variety to support
student research needs.
Strongly Agree 30% 39% 34% 37% 38% 50%
Agree 52% 42% 51% 47% 52% 42%
Neutral 8% 14% 6% 4% 5% 2%
Disagree 4% 1% 2% 2%
Strongly Disagree 2% 2%
Don't Know or
N/A 4% 6% 8% 5% 5% 3%
Last but not least, the Library is also home to the Mount Shasta Collection which is the largest known
repository of information and documents about Mount Shasta. The collection consists of thousands of
books, articles, manuscripts, photographs, maps, prints, and audiovisual materials about the Mount
Shasta volcano and surrounding area. While not used often by students, the collection receives steady
use by community members and other researchers. The Library frequently collaborates with the Sisson
Museum in Mount Shasta and occasionally with the Siskiyou County Museum in Yreka by providing
research assistance and display materials. In the last four years, the Library has hosted a variety of non-
local researchers which have or will result in publications such as a creative writing dissertation,
conference presentations, articles, books, and a TV episode. The Mount Shasta Collection has not grown
significantly in recent years as staff numbers and budget shrink and further discussions on its value to
the college and the appropriate level of human and funding resources to devote to it are needed.
Research Services
Our collections serve as the backbone for our reference services. For reporting purposes, a reference
transaction is defined as:
Information contacts that involve the knowledge, use, recommendations, interpretation, or
instruction in the use of one or more information sources by a member of the library staff…
Information sources include printed, online, and non-printed materials, e-resources and
databases (including assistance with computer searching), the library’s own catalogs and other
holdings records, other libraries and institutions through communication or referral, and
persons both inside and outside the library.9
8 Figures used for these general calculations are from the Library’s IPEDS data for AY14-15 through AY17-18
9 ACRL Academic Library Trends and Statistics: Annual Survey: Instructions and Definitions. ACRL, Aug. 2019.
https://acrl.countingopinions.com/docs/acrl/2019_Survey_Instructions.pdf

-- 11 of 24 --

title: AY19-20 Library Comprehensive Program Review-Dec2019 (Part 12)
text:
12
AY10-11 AY11-12 AY12-13 AY13-14 AY14-15 AY15-16 AY16-17 AY17-18 AY18-19
reference
transactions 1,050 640 561 493 574 493 393 467 277
Reference questions have been gradually declining in the Library. Some of the recent decline may be
due to an increase in reference questions being handled in the classroom. Hands-on research sessions
have become popular and are conducted with multiple classes each semester. The small increase in
AY17-18 may have been due to the implementation of a revised and simpler reference statistics tracking
system. Unfortunately, tracking manual daily statistics became more burdensome in AY18-19 with the
implementation of TutorTrac, which requires staff to now manually enter community use of the Library.
Community members do not have TutorTrac accounts but we do not want their usage to go uncounted.
Library staff managed to record 668 community member visits to the Library in TutorTrac; no doubt
there were many more that were uncounted. It is worth considering switching to reference statistics
sampling weeks that can be extrapolated, a common library practice, in order to lighten staff load. It is
also worth noting that in addition to reference transactions, library staff fields (and attempts to track) a
variety of other questions as well such as campus directions, campus contact info, and computer,
printing, copier, and scanner help.
Use of Our Physical Space
While use of the physical library space has declined since 2010, use in the last few years has started a
slow climb from the low in AY16-17.
AY10-11 AY11-12 AY12-13 AY13-14 AY14-15 AY15-16 AY16-17 AY17-18 AY18-19
gate count 51,784 40,279 32,957 26,334 20,944 23,277 16,127 17,877 19,778
Starting with the Fall 2017 semester, the Library has stayed open until 7:00 p.m. on Mondays and
Wednesdays. These are our first evening hours in approximately eight years. While use has been light,
students often express their appreciation for these evening hours. In general, most of the use is
between 5:00 – 6:00. We had more favorable responses on our Spring 2018 Annual Student Survey to
the question “The library provides adequate access to its services and collections. (Consider things like
our hours, Internet access, and service to online and distance users)”: 91% of students stated that they
“strongly agreed” or “agreed” (only 2% disagreed). This number is up from previous years: 78% in 2017,
77% in 2016, and 83% in 2015. 2018 was also the first time many years that we did not get written
comments regarding evening hours (2019 had one comment from a Lodge student that even later hours
“would be cool”). We do still get requests for weekend hours.
In addition to adding evening hours, the Library transformed the “loft” area into the Library Lounge,
moving more armchairs into the space, allowing food and drink, and encouraging its use a gathering
space. The Lounge has also started to serve as an event space for student club movie nights and Writers
Club events. All of these changes may have helped to draw people into the Library.

-- 12 of 24 --

title: AY19-20 Library Comprehensive Program Review-Dec2019 (Part 13)
text:
13
The Library started to use TutorTrac in Fall of 2018. TutorTrac is a sign-in kiosk that asks students to log
in using their student number and then select the class and reason that prompted their visit. Students
also have some generic selections if they choose not to specify. Since the Library also welcomes
community members, staff manually enters their data into TutorTrac in order to have their visits
counted in our overall use totals. Community members are tracked using generic accounts so data
cannot be individualized; staff does enter a specific reason for the visit whenever possible.
TutorTrac shows that there were 9,228 logins from July 1, 2018 – June 30, 2019, our first full year using
this login system. Our gate count for the same time period was 19,778. Both of these statistics have
their flaws:
• Gate counts tend to overcount the number of users (it simply counts the number of bodies
walking through the entry gates and does not factor in people who may come and go during the
course of their library visit to take phone calls, restroom breaks, etc.)
• TutorTrac is expected to undercount since not all students remember to log in and staff may not
always notice a community member who comes in
TutorTrac does give us more info than we have had in the past about use of the Library and it has been
helpful to analyze it and compare it to our Annual Student Survey data. The Library has asked a question
on our Annual Student Survey since Spring 2016 about students’ main use of the library, asking them to
choose their “main use of the COS Library”; online databases, reserve textbooks, check out books,
computers/printing, study space, or other.
The Annual Student Survey collects surveys in the Library and from around the Weed Campus (Lodges,
Student Center), from Yreka Campus, and online, so not all survey respondents necessarily come into
the Library. It is interesting to note that looking solely at responses gathered on the Weed Campus,
students who had access to the Library’s physical space ranked “quiet study space” second over “online
databases,” a rankings reversal from total responses. This seems reasonable since online and Yreka
students may have limited opportunities to visit the Library in person.

TutorTrac asks similar questions, making comparisons between its results and the Annual Student
Survey useful. Comparing AY18-19 TutorTrac responses to the results from surveys collected only in the
Library in Spring 2019 clearly shows that students who do use the physical library space are doing so
primarily because of access to computers, printing, and quiet study areas.

-- 13 of 24 --

title: AY19-20 Library Comprehensive Program Review-Dec2019 (Part 14)
text:
14
Our Annual Student Survey, which is collected from students both inside and outside of the Library,
shows that that “online database” are a valued part of the Library. The fact that research/online
databases is ranked much lower by in-person library visitors indicates that students are primarily doing
research online from elsewhere and indicates that the Library should perhaps focus on expanding the
online self-serve help that we provide students.

responses)

rank

Books/Textbooks = 793 4 2 (tie) 3 Check out books + Reserve textbooks = 33 / 11

Study = 3,353 2 3 2 Quiet study space = 32 / 16

It should be noted that the TutorTrac statistics used here also include community member use which
account for 7% of logged sessions. About 61% of community members visit the Library to use our
computers; 15% for newspapers, magazines, and books; 11% to use the wifi; 6% for other reasons (“take
a break”); 5% to conduct research; and 3% to simply print.
TutorTrac data shows that most of the students who use our physical space are repeat users (76.4%),
which suggests a certain level of library satisfaction. Over a fifth (21.2%) of our student visitors are
“super users” who visited the Library twenty or more times between July 1, 2018 – June 30, 2019. The
average number of visits for super users was 44, with users at the high end visiting over 100 times (our
highest number of visits by one student was 173!). Interestingly, super users’ primary use of the Library
was overwhelmingly to study, with computer use coming well in second. Perhaps this 2016 Annual
Student Survey comment is from a super user: “I do more homework the more time I spend in the
library.” Comments on the Annual Student Survey and in the Library’s suggestion box often mention
appreciation for the Library as a quiet study space.

Students have been increasingly happy with our physical space. Three of our Annual Student Survey
questions are tied to our space and cover everything from equipment to access to comfort.
Some improvements we have made in the past four years, many based on student feedback, include:
• Increased access by adding evening hours from 5:00 – 7:00 p.m. on Mondays and Wednesdays,
starting with Fall 2017
• Eliminating library cards and our making off-campus database login the same as mySiskiyous
• Working with IT to install a double-sided printer (a frequent student request)
• Purchasing a new digital microfilm reader machine
• Purchasing of some new furniture (including computer desk task chairs and armchairs)
• Moving the staff service desk to create a more welcoming space when students first enter the
Library. This space is used for displays and as a “new book” area.

title: AY19-20 Library Comprehensive Program Review-Dec2019 (Part 16)

• Transforming the Library’s “loft” area into the Library Lounge by reducing the collections stored
there, relocating some additional armchairs, allowing food and drinks, and designating it a non-
quiet area to encourage gatherings and conversation.
• Borrowing three ASC laptops set up with Proctorio for online student test proctoring
By comparing the ethnic breakdown of students using the physical library space (based on TutorTrac
logins) to the ethnic breakdown of the campus as whole, it is obvious that the Library is not serving
our Hispanic students very well and needs to work on reaching this group of students better. While
still import to address, the gap is not quite as awful as the numbers suggest. The campus-wide
statistic includes FIELD students who are overwhelmingly Hispanic but well outside of our
geographic area (and, therefore, not in-person library users). The Library is doing a good job of
reaching other student populations, including other minority groups. We do not have a similar data
set to analyze for students who only experience the Library online.

The very general question about the Library on the Noel-Levitz Survey indicates that overall Library
satisfaction has been increasing during the past few years, although satisfaction is not rising quite in
step with importance. We will be watching these survey results to see if rankings and gaps continue to
improve.

title: AY19-20 Library Comprehensive Program Review-Dec2019 (Part 18)
text:
18
g) Staffing
• Discussed in 1. Description of Program
• Follow-up action addressed in all aspects of 9. Four-Year Action Plan
h) Participation in shared governance
All staff participate in Planning Day activities. Marcia and Linda feel there are adequate
opportunities to participate in shared governance and campus decision-making but, thus far,
have declined the opportunity to participate on campus committees. It is too soon to tell
whether or not the newly-formed Classified Senate will provide them with an avenue for greater
participation.
As Faculty Librarian, Jude participates in shared governance by attended Academic Senate
meetings and serving on Equivalency Committee (Fall 2014 – Spring 2015), Accreditation
Steering Committee, Chair of Standard IIB (August 2014 – March 2016), Program Review
Committee (August 2015 – February 2017), Distance Learning Committee (September 2016 –
current; suspended Fall 2019), Instruction Council (September 2016 – current; attend as Library
representative, not as an Academic Senate representative), 508 Compliance/ADA Workgroup
(November 2017 – current), Guided Pathways (in various iterations since Spring 2018; currently
on Pillar 3), and several hiring committees.
Library staff works collaboratively on planning and decision-making. The Library used to have
regularly scheduled staff meetings that included our administrator but we have moved away
from this in the past few years since administrator attendance has been minimal. Library staff
now meets on an as-needed basis. We would like to return to a light schedule of meetings with
our administrator once or twice a semester to supplement our internal meetings.
i) Professional development and training
The Library has invested our budget in the following professional development opportunities
and trainings since AY2014-15 13; the Library’s budget has been sufficient in this area.
• Jude: Council of Chief Librarians (CCL) Annual Dean’s Directors Meeting (2015, 2016,
2017, 2018, 2019); Copyright Workshop (American Library Association, 2016); Library
Assessment & Statistics (CCL, 2016); CCL Regional Library Meeting (2017); Libraries and
Guided Pathways (CCL, 2018); Spring Fling brief online workshops on marketing and
data literacy (California Library Association, 2018); Library Marketing (Infopeople, 2018)
• Marcia: WMS West Coast Annual Meeting (2016), Caring for Collections (Library Juice,
2016), Authority Control (Library Juice, 2016), Library Design (CCL, 2017); Intro to
Archives Administration & Management (Library Juice, 2018); Using MarcEdit (Library
Juice, 2019)
13 This list includes training paid for from the Library’s budget only. It does not include Flex and other campus-
provided activities, training paid for out of other campus funds, or training paid for with personal funds.

-- 18 of 24 --

title: AY19-20 Library Comprehensive Program Review-Dec2019 (Part 19)
text:
19
• Linda: Student Staff Development (Library Juice, 2016), Beginning Cataloging (Library
Juice, 2017); Core Reference Fundamentals (Infopeople, 2018); Designing Effective New
Employee Orientations (infopeople, 2018)
• Nancy Shepard: CCL Annual Dean’s Directors Meeting (2015, 2016)
j) Compliance with applicable mandates
The Library completes the Chancellor’s Office Library Annual Data Survey and IPEDS Library
survey as required each year.
This Program Review provides many examples of how the Library is working to meet ACCJC
Accreditation Standard IIB for providing student support “sufficient in quantity, currency, depth,
and variety, and variety to support educational programs, regardless of location or means of
delivery”; for making informed decisions regarding selection and maintenance of our resources;
and assessing whether or not our resources, services, and collaborations are meeting student
needs.
7. Vision
Four years from now…
a) The Library will have adequate staffing to meet our workload needs and provide adequate open
hours
b) The Library will have increased instructional activities, including offering a credit-bearing
Research Skills and/or Information Literacy course
c) The Library will have greater student engagement from developing a student-friendly space, by
adapting to the ways students use technology, with increased activities, and through access to
resources that meet student needs
d) The Library will have greater faculty engagement with both our space and our collections
e) The Library will have a management plan for our unique Mount Shasta Collection and for the
College History Archives
8. Progress on Prior Goals
Here are the updates on goals and projects covered in our last Annual Update in Fall 2018.
Updating Library space to be more appealing to students
• Worked with IT to assemble and purchase needed equipment to show movies in the Lounge;
purchased media cart and sound system for the Lounge
• Worked with Maintenance to hang a retractable screen in the Lounge and add window frame
hooks for removable blackout curtains for movie events
• Selected new furniture and sound panel purchases for FY18-19 but funding was frozen before
purchases could be made; ideas have been saved for FY19-20
• The Library did not conduct a focus group in Spring 2019 as planned

-- 19 of 24 --

title: AY19-20 Library Comprehensive Program Review-Dec2019 (Part 20)
text:
20
Staffing challenges
• The Library has not made progress in resolving staffing limitations; efforts such as reclassifying
one position to a higher pay rate, obtaining overtime funds to cover evening hours as needed,
and requests for adjunct funds to offer more hours during Winter/Summer sessions have been
unsuccessful. Keeping up with the demands of the LSP migration project have been challenging
and the Library is behind schedule
Banner-EZProxy integration
• After discussion with IT, this issue was tabled until after the migration to the new LSP is
complete
The Sheet Music Project
• This project was suspended when we took on the migration project; we will return to it in AY19-
20
9. Four-Year Action Plan
The Library has developed a Four-Year Action Plan based on our Vision.
a) The Library will have adequate staffing to meet our workload needs and provide
adequate open hours
The Library has been operating with a skeleton crew since the retirement of Nancy Shepard and is now
facing the retirement in Spring 2020 of our longest-serving staff member, Marcia Eblen. Nancy’s
departure left an administrative gap that is being partially filled by the faculty librarian, decreasing the
time available for instructional activities. In addition to providing much institutional history for both the
Library and the college as a whole, Marcia also handles high-level work using a skill set that will be
difficult to replace. Attempts in 2018 to reclassify her position at a higher level were unsuccessful even
though some of her workload requires specialized training and some leans towards faculty work such as
in-depth reference assistance with the Mount Shasta Collection and original cataloging. The Library has
also faced limitations on open hours during the Summer Session and Winter Intersession because the
faculty librarian only has a 195-day contract and adjunct funds are limited.
The Library needs a third person in order to maintain existing hours and to have reasonable open hours
during Summer/Winter, however, the Library needs to examine workload and determine how to best fill
Marcia’s position. Some ideas include: filling the position “as is”; filling it as a 10-month position and
putting the Faculty Librarian on a 244-day contract with the salary savings; or filling it as a 10-month
position and adding an Adjunct Librarian with the salary savings.
In the last couple of years, library staff has become burdened by statistics-keeping at the service desk.
Staff track reference statistics, tour headcounts, gate counts, community member TutorTrac logins, and
special statistics (like periodical use) in addition to providing reference assistance and other help to
students. The result is that staff cannot keep up and statistics quality suffers. Compiling this program
review has also brought to light some areas where statistics could be better aligned, such as TutorTrac
“reasons” and our Annual Student Survey “library use reasons” question.

-- 20 of 24 --

title: AY19-20 Library Comprehensive Program Review-Dec2019 (Part 21)
text:
21
Action Steps:
• Determine positions the Library needs by the end of February 2020
• Write job descriptions and submit CQIP(s) as needed
• Fill position(s) by June 30, 2020 (Marcia retires at the end of Spring 2020)
• Investigate extrapolated statistics-keeping by the end of Spring 2020; implement by Fall 2020
• Realign survey/statistics questions as needed between Annual Student Survey, TutorTrac,
Library SLOs, CCCCO Annual Data Survey, IPEDS, ACCJC Accreditation standard IIB, Noel-Levitz,
CSSE, etc. by Fall 2020
• Schedule twice-a-semester meetings with the Library’s administrator to aid communication
b) The Library will have increased instructional activities for students, including offering a
credit-bearing Research Skills and/or Information Literacy course
Database use statistics are very high while students claiming to come to the Library for research
purposes is low (as are reference statistics), indicating that students are doing their research online and
on their own. While the Library has contact with a large number of students through class visits, tours,
and other activities, many students still never receive direct instruction or interact with staff. Research
skills are important for student success in many classes and have the potential to be a marketable job
skill. Library staffing is often an obstacle for expanding instructional activities.
Action Steps:
• Update existing instructional materials. Materials related to the library catalog need to updated
and replaced in Spring 2020. Develop a project list of other needed or anticipated updates by
the end of Spring 2020 with target dates for completion.
• Submit curriculum for at least one credit-bearing class by January 31, 2021
• Student employee supervisor will complete local Canvas training by June 30, 2020
• Expand student employee training program in Canvas to include a Customer Service badge by
December 31, 2020
• Investigate whether or not the Library can access individual use statistics for our online
collections with the new LSP, the switch to single sign-on, or using the new EZproxy Analytics
product (this data is not currently available to us). Learning more about student use of online
materials may shine a light on other areas where we can improve
• Revise the Library’s SLOs
• Look for ways this general goal might support Guided Pathways efforts on campus
c) The Library will have greater student engagement from developing a student-friendly
space, from adapting to the ways students use technology, from increased activities, and
through access to resources that meets student needs
With over 20% of our users visiting the Library an average of 44 times, many students clearly love our
space! These library “super users” as well as Annual Student Survey feedback consistently indicate that
they appreciate the Library as a quiet study space. The Library would benefit from learning about what
“regular” students would like from our space and balance this with what is already working for many

-- 21 of 24 --

title: AY19-20 Library Comprehensive Program Review-Dec2019 (Part 22)
text:
22
students. More marketing of our space and resources, both in their current form and as they evolve, is
also important.
Action Steps
General
• Conduct a student focus group about ways the library can better engage with and support
students, including Library hours during all semesters
• Refresh marketing materials promoting the Library space, collections, and resources
• Look for ways this general goal might tie into Institutional Master Plan Focus Area 3
(Engagement) and might support Guided Pathways efforts on campus
• Research academic library student engagement to generate new ideas
• Maintain AY2018-19 gate count in AY2019-20. Increase gate count and the number of unique
student visitors (TutorTrac) by 3% in each subsequent academic year. Work with the ASC to re-
allow EDUC 0670 students to study in the Library14

Target # of unique
student visitors
9,228 9,505 9,790 10,084 10,387
Develop a student-friendly space
• Continue to replace old, uncomfortable furniture with new furniture
• Give our aging space a boost with fresh paint in certain areas such as the window bays, window
sills, railings and the study room
• Develop LRC 9 into a multipurpose technology room that can be used as an overflow
study/testing room (and can still be combined with LRC 8 for large groups)
• Determine ways the library can be zoned to have both quiet study areas as well as areas that do
away with the “hushed” stereotype in order to meet a variety of student study preferences;
consider enclosed study cubbies or small study rooms (for solo quiet), and/or a glass barrier wall
for the Lounge
• Investigate how to better reach Hispanic students on campus in order to increase their use of
our space
Adapting to the ways student use technology
• Investigate student preferences and implement a way to contact the Library by text, chat, or
other methods
14 In the past, students enrolled in “athletic study hall” have had the option of spending that time period in the

option. The Library is working with the ASC to determine if this can be allowed again while still being in compliance
with Chancellor’s Office requirements for EDUC 0670.

-- 22 of 24 --

title: AY19-20 Library Comprehensive Program Review-Dec2019 (Part 23)
text:
23
• Aquire more laptops; when desktop computers are due for replacement, consider replacing
some with laptops (current library computers were installed in 2014)
• Aquire Chromebooks specifically for Proctorio testing
• Aquire portable chargers for phones and other devices. Possibly include ones for laptops too
since there is a shortage of electrical outlets
• Investigate student preferences about a computer workstation that can be both wheelchair
accessible and serve as a standing workstation
Increased activities
• Increase the amount of Student Club use of the library
• Start offering Library-sponsored activities such as speakers, movie nights, craft/maker activities,
game nights, local history exhibits, and student art shows
• Reassess current technology used for movie nights (some of the technology is old and has been
problematic)
• Consider offering “special occasion” hours such as during Thanksgiving and Spring Breaks and
the Sunday before finals
• Participate in the Student Success Seminars again (if the ASC offers this workshop series);
considering holding classes in the Library study room
• Increase awareness of library services on the Yreka Campus
d) Increase faculty engagement with the library
The Library would like to strengthen our partnership with all discipline faculty to better support our
curriculum and students. The Library often reaches out to faculty with email updates, information in
newsletters such as Campus Connection and Dean Peretti’s LASt Word, and with direct appeals (visiting a
faculty member in person, presentations at Academic Senate or other meetings, etc.). Faculty feedback
is taken seriously and the Library has often implemented their suggestions such as reserves on Yreka
Campus, starting a JSTOR database subscription, and bringing back interlibrary loan services (ILL) in
2014. Unfortunately, these efforts alone do not seem to be working well: faculty rarely come to the
Library and their use of our physical collections and services like ILL are low. The extent of their use of
our helpful Services to Faculty site, our online collections and our instructional materials such as video
tutorials and Canvas modules is unknown. Library staffing may be an obstacle to completing some of
these activities.
Action Steps
• Investigate the possibility of transforming LRC 9 into a multipurpose technology room that can
be used as a media production space for faculty (among other uses)
• Create additional instructional materials to be posted online and in Canvas. Develop a project
list by the end of Spring 2020 with target dates for completion.
• Create an online flex activity for faculty that teaches them about library collections and services
by the start of January 1, 2021
• Offer updates and presentations on our new LSP “discovery system” in Spring 2020

-- 23 of 24 --

title: AY19-20 Library Comprehensive Program Review-Dec2019 (Part 24)
text:
24
• Participate in any training offered for adjunct faculty, either online or in person
• Determine faculty library needs through a general survey, focus group, or reintroduction of an
Advisory Committee
• Market the Library space to faculty such as for meetings and adjunct work space
• Look for ways to collaborate with faculty on specific projects that will benefit their area (as
Library time/staffing allow). Finish the current project with the Music faculty
• Investigate ways we can collect better use statistics of online collections and resources by
faculty
• Start regular meetings with the ASC Coordinator and Basecamp Coordinator to better support
each area’s efforts and increase communication within the LRC areas directly serving students
• Look for ways this general goal might support Guided Pathways efforts on campus
e) Improve our collections
Much of the Library’s work revolves around our collections, from cataloging and book checkouts to web
site content to reference and instruction. While student satisfaction with our collections has been going
up, there is still room for improvement. The more use our collections get, the better the value for the
college. The Library also maintains several collections more for the benefit of the college than for
students: the Mount Shasta Collection and the college “archives” which preserves materials important
to college history. Library staffing may be an obstacle to completing some of these activities.
Action Steps
• Include collection development policies in AP4040 and have AP4040 approved by the Board by
June 2020
• Create a collection development plan for the college “archives”
• Create a collection plan for the Mount Shasta Collection that takes into account staffing and
budget considerations
• Grow collaboration with Siskiyou Count County Libraries to leverage our collections
• Research the need for and determine if funding is available for a science database
• Increase book circulation
• Look for new ways to track/analyze print circulation: is purchasing print worth the budget?
• Increase use of Yreka Reserves
• Look for ways this general goal might support Guided Pathways efforts on campus
10. Conclusion
The COS Library is doing well. We are fulfilling our mission to support student learning and our college
employees in a variety of ways. In doing so, we also support the mission of the college and are meeting
accreditation requirements. While we are doing well, the Library has identified areas where we can
improve, adapt to changing times and technology, and continue to grow stronger so that we can
continue to meet our campus community’s needs in the first half of the 2020’s.

-- 24 of 24 --
